nuggets.lo_heading: F-A-010. Cell division and Chromosomal abnormalities
content: The **Chromosome Theory of Inheritance** posits that genes are located on specific loci of chromosomes and their behavior during meiosis explains inheritance patterns.
**Mitosis** occurs in somatic cells and involves one division resulting in two **genetically identical diploid (2n)** daughter cells.
**Meiosis** occurs in germ cells, involving two divisions (Meiosis I and II) to produce four **genetically unique haploid (1n)** gametes.
**Prophase I** of meiosis is unique for **crossover** (pachynema stage), which increases genetic variability.
**Nondisjunction** is the failure of homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids to separate, leading to **aneuploidy**.
**Numerical chromosomal anomalies** include **trisomy** (extra chromosome) and **monosomy** (missing chromosome).
The most common cause of **Trisomy 21 (Down Syndrome)** is maternal meiotic nondisjunction, often correlated with **advanced maternal age**.
Clinical features of **Down Syndrome** include **hypotonia**, **simian crease**, **flat facial profile**, and **up-slanting palpebral fissures**.
**Trisomy 18 (Edwards Syndrome)** is characterized by **clenched fists with overlapping fingers**, **rocker-bottom feet**, and **micrognathia**.
**Trisomy 13 (Patau Syndrome)** presents with **holoprosencephaly**, **polydactyly**, and **cleft lip/palate**.
**Turner Syndrome (45,X)** is the only viable monosomy, presenting with **webbed neck**, **short stature**, and **streak ovaries**.
**Klinefelter Syndrome (47,XXY)** is characterized by **testicular atrophy**, **gynecomastia**, and **eunuchoid body habitus**.
**Structural abnormalities** result from chromosome breakage, including **translocations**, **deletions**, and **inversions**.
**Cri-du-chat syndrome** is caused by a partial deletion of the **short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-)**.
**Angelman Syndrome** involves a microdeletion on the **maternal chromosome 15**, leading to 'happy puppet' features.
**Prader-Willi Syndrome** involves a microdeletion on the **paternal chromosome 15**, leading to **hyperphagia** and obesity.
**Mosaicism** occurs when two or more cell lines with different genotypes exist in one individual, caused by **post-zygotic mitotic nondisjunction**.
**Teratomas** are tumors containing tissues from all three germ layers; they often arise from **pluripotent stem cells** or **primordial germ cells**.
**Gene Mutations** are permanent alterations in the DNA sequence; **point mutations** involve a single nucleotide change.
**Karyotyping** is the gold standard for detecting large-scale numerical and structural chromosomal abnormalities.
**Fluorescence In Situ Hybridization (FISH)** utilizes fluorescent probes to detect specific DNA sequences or microdeletions.
**Microarray analysis** is used to detect **copy number variants (CNVs)** that are too small to be seen on a standard karyotype.
**Meiosis I** is a reductional division; **Meiosis II** is an equational division similar to mitosis.
**Synapsis** is the pairing of homologous chromosomes during prophase I, mediated by the **synaptonemal complex**.
**Disomy** refers to the presence of two copies of a chromosome; **uniparental disomy** is when both copies come from one parent.
**Balanced translocations** usually do not result in a phenotype but increase the risk of abnormal gametes.
**Robertsonian translocation** involves the fusion of the long arms of two **acrocentric chromosomes** (e.g., 14 and 21).
**Anaphase lag** is a mechanism of mosaicism where a chromosome fails to incorporate into the nucleus of a daughter cell.
**Polyploidy** refers to extra sets of chromosomes (e.g., **triploidy 69,XXX**), often resulting from **dispermy**.
**Barr bodies** represent inactivated X chromosomes; the number of Barr bodies is always **n-1** of the total X chromosomes.
**Fragile X syndrome** is caused by triplet repeat expansion (**CGG**) in the **FMR1 gene**.
**Amniocentesis** is a prenatal diagnostic technique usually performed between **15-20 weeks** of gestation.
**Chorionic Villus Sampling (CVS)** allows for earlier genetic testing, typically between **10-12 weeks**.
**Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT)** analyzes **cell-free fetal DNA** in maternal blood for aneuploidy screening.
**Germline mutations** are heritable, whereas **somatic mutations** are not passed to offspring.
**Nonsense mutations** create a premature stop codon, leading to a truncated protein.
**Missense mutations** result in a different amino acid being incorporated into the protein.
**Frameshift mutations** occur due to insertions or deletions not in multiples of three, altering the reading frame.
The **clinical justification** for trisomy phenotypes often involves **gene dosage effects**.
**Sacrococcygeal teratomas** are the most common tumors in newborns, derived from remnants of the **primitive streak**.
**Spermatogenesis** is the process by which **spermatogonia** are transformed into mature spermatozoa.
The process begins at **puberty** within the **seminiferous tubules** of the testes.
**Type A spermatogonia** serve as stem cells that maintain the germ cell population.
**Type B spermatogonia** undergo mitosis to give rise to **primary spermatocytes**.
**Primary spermatocytes** undergo Meiosis I (a prolonged prophase) to form **secondary spermatocytes**.
**Secondary spermatocytes** quickly undergo Meiosis II to produce four haploid **spermatids**.
**Spermiogenesis** is the final phase where spermatids transform into motile **spermatozoa** without further cell division.
The **Acrosome formation** involves the Golgi apparatus producing enzymes (acrosin, hyaluronidase) for egg penetration.
**Condensation of the nucleus** occurs during spermiogenesis, replacing histones with **protamines**.
The **Flagellum (tail)** formation involves the centrioles and provides motility.
The **Mitochondrial sheath** organizes in the **middle piece** of the tail to provide ATP.
**Sertoli cells** (nurse cells) support, protect, and nourish the developing germ cells.
The **Blood-testis barrier** is formed by tight junctions between Sertoli cells to protect germ cells from the immune system.
**Leydig cells** (interstitial cells) produce **testosterone** under the influence of **Luteinizing Hormone (LH)**.
**Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH)** acts on Sertoli cells to stimulate spermatogenesis and **androgen-binding protein (ABP)** production.
The entire process of spermatogenesis takes approximately **64 to 74 days** in humans.
**Spermiation** is the release of mature spermatozoa from Sertoli cells into the lumen of seminiferous tubules.
**Abnormal sperm** can be **morphological** (two heads, giant heads) or **motility-based** (asthenozoospermia).
**Oligozoospermia** refers to a low sperm count (less than 15 million/mL).
**Azoospermia** is the total absence of sperm in the ejaculate.
**Teratozoospermia** refers to a high percentage of sperm with abnormal morphology.
Spermatozoa are stored and undergo further maturation (gaining motility) in the **epididymis**.
**Nondisjunction** during spermatogenesis is a significant cause of sex chromosome aneuploidies like **Klinefelter syndrome**.
**Spermatogonia** are situated at the **basal compartment** of the seminiferous epithelium.
**Primary spermatocytes** are the largest germ cells seen in the seminiferous tubule sections.
The **endpiece** of the sperm tail contains only the **axoneme** covered by the plasma membrane.
**Residual bodies** are portions of cytoplasm shed by spermatids and phagocytosed by Sertoli cells.
**Inhibin** produced by Sertoli cells provides negative feedback to the anterior pituitary to inhibit **FSH**.
**Testosterone** provides negative feedback to inhibit **GnRH** and **LH**.
Spermatozoa entering the epididymis are **not yet motile**.
The **Acrosome** covers the anterior two-thirds of the sperm nucleus.
**Spermatozoon** consists of a head (nucleus + acrosome) and a tail (middle, principal, and end pieces).
The **Principal piece** is the longest portion of the tail.
**Seminal fluid** provides nutrients (fructose) and an alkaline environment to neutralize vaginal acidity.
**Prostate-specific antigen (PSA)** helps in the liquefaction of the seminal coagulum.
**Dynein arms** in the axoneme are essential for the ciliary movement of the sperm tail.
Genetic causes of abnormal gametes include **microdeletions on the Y chromosome** (AZF region).
Environmental factors like **radiation** and **chemotherapy** drastically impair spermatogenesis.
**Varicocele** (dilation of pampiniform plexus) increases scrotal temperature, leading to poor sperm quality.
**Cryptorchidism** (undescended testes) leads to sterility because spermatogenesis requires a temperature lower than body core.
The **Female Reproductive Cycle** is governed by the **Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Ovarian axis**.
**GnRH** from the hypothalamus stimulates the anterior pituitary to release **FSH** and **LH**.
The **Ovarian Cycle** consists of the **follicular phase**, **ovulation**, and the **luteal phase**.
**FSH** stimulates the growth of 15–20 **primary follicles**, but usually only one reaches maturity (Graafian follicle).
**Estrogen** is produced by the **theca interna** and **granulosa cells** of the growing follicles.
High levels of estrogen exert **positive feedback** on the pituitary, leading to the **LH surge**.
The **LH surge** is the direct trigger for **ovulation**, occurring 12–24 hours after the peak.
**Ovulation** involves the release of the **secondary oocyte** (arrested in Metaphase II) from the follicle.
The **Stigma** is the small, avascular area on the ovary surface that ruptures during ovulation.
**Mittelschmerz** refers to mid-cycle abdominal pain associated with ovulation.
Following ovulation, the remaining follicle walls collapse and form the **Corpus Luteum**.
The **Corpus Luteum** primarily secretes **progesterone** to prepare the endometrium for implantation.
If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates into the **Corpus Albicans** (fibrous scar).
If fertilization occurs, **Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG)** prevents the degeneration of the corpus luteum.
The **Uterine (Menstrual) Cycle** consists of the **menstrual phase**, **proliferative phase**, and **secretory phase**.
The **Menstrual phase** (Day 1-5) involves the shedding of the **functional layer** of the endometrium.
The **Proliferative phase** (Day 6-14) is driven by **estrogen** and involves re-epithelialization of the endometrium.
The **Secretory phase** (Day 15-28) is driven by **progesterone**, characterized by tortuous glands and glycogen storage.
The **Ischemic phase** occurs just before menstruation due to the withdrawal of progesterone causing spiral artery constriction.
The **Basal layer** of the endometrium is not shed and serves to regenerate the functional layer.
**Cervical mucus** becomes thin, watery, and alkaline (forming a 'ferning' pattern) under estrogen to facilitate sperm entry.
**Cervical mucus** becomes thick and acidic under progesterone, forming a 'plug' to prevent infection.
The **Theca interna** cells possess LH receptors and produce androgens.
**Aromatase** in granulosa cells converts androgens to estrogens.
**Anovulatory cycles** are common in the first few years after menarche and before menopause.
The **Secondary Oocyte** is surrounded by the **zona pellucida** and **corona radiata** at the time of ovulation.
**Follicular atresia** is the process where non-dominant follicles degenerate.
The **Antrum** is the fluid-filled cavity within a secondary (antral) follicle.
**Cumulus oophorus** is the group of granulosa cells surrounding the oocyte that projects into the antrum.
The **Corpus Luteum of Pregnancy** remains active for about 20 weeks until the placenta takes over hormone production.
**Luteolysis** is the breakdown of the corpus luteum when pregnancy does not occur.
**Prostaglandins** (PGF2-alpha) play a role in uterine contractions and corpus luteum regression.
The **Ampulla** of the uterine tube is the most common site for fertilization.
**Primary oocytes** are arrested in **Prophase I (Diplotene stage)** from fetal life until puberty.
**Meiosis I** is completed just before ovulation, resulting in a secondary oocyte and the **first polar body**.
**Meiosis II** is only completed if **fertilization** occurs, resulting in a mature ovum and a **second polar body**.
**Theca externa** is the outer fibrous layer of the developing follicle.
**Estrogen** levels peak approximately 24 hours before ovulation.
**Progesterone** levels peak during the mid-luteal phase (approximately day 21).
The **functional layer** of the endometrium is composed of the **stratum compactum** and **stratum spongiosum**.
**Fertilization** is the sequence of events beginning with contact between a sperm and an oocyte and ending with the intermingling of chromosomes.
**Capacitation** is a 7-hour period of conditioning in the female tract where the **glycoprotein coat** is removed from the sperm's acrosome.
Only **capacitated sperm** can pass through the corona radiata and undergo the acrosome reaction.
The **Acrosome Reaction** involves the release of enzymes like **acrosin** to digest the zona pellucida.
Binding to the **ZP3 protein** on the zona pellucida is the critical trigger for the acrosome reaction.
**Phase 1** of fertilization: Penetration of the **corona radiata** by capacitated sperm.
**Phase 2** of fertilization: Penetration of the **zona pellucida**.
**Phase 3** of fertilization: Fusion of the **oocyte and sperm cell membranes**.
The **Cortical Reaction** involves the release of lysosomal granules from the oocyte to prevent **polyspermy**.
The **Zona Reaction** makes the zona pellucida impermeable to other sperm by altering its chemical properties.
Upon sperm entry, the oocyte completes **Meiosis II**, forming a mature ovum and the **second polar body**.
The **Female Pronucleus** is the decondensed nucleus of the mature ovum.
The **Male Pronucleus** is formed as the sperm nucleus swells; the tail is usually detached and degenerates.
**Results of Fertilization** include the restoration of the **diploid (2n)** number of chromosomes.
The **genetic sex** of the embryo is determined at fertilization by the X or Y chromosome of the sperm.
**Variation** of the human species is achieved through the mingling of maternal and paternal chromosomes.
**Metabolic activation** of the egg occurs, initiating the first cleavage division.
The **Zygote** is the unicellular embryo formed at the end of fertilization.
Fertilization typically occurs in the **ampulla** of the uterine tube within **12–24 hours** after ovulation.
If the oocyte is not fertilized, it degenerates and is phagocytosed within 24 hours.
**Hyaluronidase** is an enzyme released from the acrosome to disperse corona radiata cells.
**Triploidy** (69 chromosomes) can occur if two sperm fertilize one egg (**dispermy**).
The **Integrins** on the oocyte surface and **disintegrins** on the sperm surface mediate the fusion of membranes.
After membrane fusion, both the **head and tail** of the sperm enter the oocyte cytoplasm, but the plasma membrane remains behind.
The **oocyte becomes impenetrable** to other sperm almost instantly after the first sperm fuses.
**Cleavage** begins approximately 30 hours after fertilization.
The **first cleavage** division results in two daughter cells called **blastomeres**.
**Infertility** is often diagnosed if a couple fails to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse.
**Sperm counts** below 10 million/mL are generally considered a high risk for male infertility.
The movement of sperm through the cervix is assisted by **prostaglandins** in the semen.
**Sperm viability** in the female tract is usually 48 to 72 hours, while the oocyte is viable for about 24 hours.
The zygote is **totipotent**, meaning it can give rise to any cell type and extraembryonic tissues.
**Blastomeres** are surrounded by the zona pellucida until the blastocyst stage.
The **Polar body** serves as a marker for the animal pole of the oocyte.
Fertilization marks the beginning of **Day 1** of human development.
**Oophorectomy** (removal of ovaries) or tubal ligation are surgical methods to prevent fertilization.
**Ectopic pregnancy** can occur if the zygote implants outside the uterus, often due to delayed transport in the uterine tube.
The **pronuclei** replicate their DNA before they fuse.
The breakdown of the pronuclear membranes leads to the first mitotic spindle formation.
**Sperm chemotaxis** involves the oocyte releasing chemicals to attract sperm.
**Week 2** of development is often called the 'Week of Twos' because many structures double.
The **Trophoblast** differentiates into two layers: the inner **Cytotrophoblast** and the outer **Syncytiotrophoblast**.
The **Embryoblast** (inner cell mass) differentiates into two layers: the **Epiblast** and the **Hypoblast**.
The **Bilaminar Germ Disc** is composed of the epiblast and hypoblast layers.
The **Syncytiotrophoblast** is a multinucleated mass that invades the uterine endometrium by secreting **proteolytic enzymes**.
**Implantation** typically begins around **Day 6** and is completed by the end of the second week.
The **Amniotic cavity** forms as a small space within the **epiblast**.
**Amnioblasts** are epiblast cells adjacent to the cytotrophoblast that line the amniotic cavity.
The **Hypoblast** cells migrate to line the blastocyst cavity, forming the **Exocoelomic (Heuser's) membrane**.
The blastocyst cavity is renamed the **Exocoelomic cavity** or **Primary Umbilical Vesicle (Yolk Sac)**.
**Trophoblastic lacunae** appear in the syncytiotrophoblast, which later fill with maternal blood.
The **Extraembryonic Mesoderm** develops between the cytotrophoblast and the primary yolk sac.
Large cavities form in the extraembryonic mesoderm, which coalesce to form the **Extraembryonic Coelom (Chorionic Cavity)**.
The extraembryonic mesoderm splits into two layers: **Somatic** (lining cytotrophoblast) and **Splanchnic** (covering yolk sac).
The **Chorion** is formed by the extraembryonic somatic mesoderm and the two layers of trophoblast.
The **Chorionic Sac** contains the embryo, amniotic sac, and yolk sac suspended by the **connecting stalk**.
The **Connecting stalk** is the precursor to the **umbilical cord**.
**Decidua reaction** refers to endometrial cells becoming swollen with glycogen and lipids to provide nutrients.
**Abnormal implantation** sites include the **pouch of Douglas**, **mesentery**, or **ovary** (ectopic pregnancies).
**Tubal pregnancy** is the most common type of ectopic pregnancy, usually occurring in the **ampulla**.
**Placenta Previa** occurs when the blastocyst implants in the lower uterine segment, covering the internal os.
**Molar Pregnancy (Hydatidiform Mole)** involves trophoblastic hyperplasia with little or no embryonic tissue.
**Complete Moles** are usually **46,XX** and contain only paternal chromosomes (androgenesis).
**High levels of hCG** are diagnostic markers for molar pregnancies and choriocarcinomas.
The **Prechordal Plate** is a localized thickening of hypoblast cells indicating the future **cranial** region.
**Primary Villi** are finger-like projections of cytotrophoblast that grow into the syncytiotrophoblast.
By **Day 13**, the primary yolk sac is pinched off, and the **Secondary (Definitive) Umbilical Vesicle** is formed.
The **Closing plug** (fibrin coagulum) seals the penetration site in the endometrial epithelium.
**Implantation bleeding** can occur around Day 28 of the cycle, sometimes mistaken for a light period.
The **Syncytiotrophoblast** produces **hCG**, which is detectable in maternal urine by the end of week 2.
**Extraembryonic somatic mesoderm** lines the interior of the cytotrophoblast.
**Extraembryonic splanchnic mesoderm** surrounds the yolk sac.
The **Chorionic Plate** is the layer of extraembryonic mesoderm lining the inside of the chorion.
Implantation usually occurs on the **posterior or anterior wall** of the uterine body.
**Aneuploidy** is the most common cause of spontaneous abortion during the second week.
The **Epiblast** forms the floor of the amniotic cavity.
The **Hypoblast** forms the roof of the primary umbilical vesicle.
**Vaginal ultrasonography** can detect the chorionic sac by the end of the second week.
The **Trophoblastic lacunae** establish the primitive **utero-placental circulation**.
**Choriocarcinoma** is a malignant tumor arising from the trophoblast.
In the **Anatomical Position**, the person stands erect, eyes looking forward, upper limbs by the sides, and **palms facing forward**.
The **Median Plane** (Midsagittal) is a vertical plane passing longitudinally through the center of the body, dividing it into equal **right and left halves**.
**Sagittal Planes** are vertical planes passing through the body parallel to the median plane; they are often called **paramedian planes**.
**Frontal (Coronal) Planes** are vertical planes passing through the body at right angles to the median plane, dividing the body into **anterior (front)** and **posterior (back)** parts.
**Transverse (Axial) Planes** are horizontal planes passing through the body at right angles to the median and frontal planes, dividing it into **superior (upper)** and **inferior (lower)** parts.
The term **Anterior (Ventral)** denotes the front surface of the body, while **Posterior (Dorsal)** denotes the back surface.
**Superior (Cranial)** refers to a structure being nearer to the vertex (top of the skull), while **Inferior (Caudal)** refers to a structure nearer to the sole of the foot.
**Medial** indicates that a structure is nearer to the **median plane** of the body.
**Lateral** indicates that a structure is farther away from the **median plane**.
**Proximal** is used in limbs to denote a position nearer to the **trunk** or point of origin.
**Distal** is used in limbs to denote a position farther from the **trunk** or point of origin.
**Superficial** means nearer to the surface, while **Deep** means farther from the body surface.
**Ipsilateral** refers to something occurring on the **same side** of the body (e.g., right hand and right foot).
**Contralateral** refers to occurring on the **opposite side** of the body (e.g., right hand and left hand).
The **Palmar** surface refers to the anterior aspect of the hand, equivalent to the palm.
The **Plantar** surface refers to the inferior aspect (sole) of the foot.
**Flexion** involves bending or decreasing the angle between the bones or parts of the body.
**Extension** involves straightening or increasing the angle between the bones or parts of the body.
**Abduction** is moving a limb away from the **midline** of the body in the frontal plane.
**Adduction** is moving a limb toward the **midline** of the body in the frontal plane.
**Medial Rotation** brings the anterior surface of a limb closer to the median plane.
**Lateral Rotation** takes the anterior surface of a limb away from the median plane.
**Pronation** is a rotational movement of the forearm where the palm faces **posteriorly**.
**Supination** is a rotational movement of the forearm where the palm faces **anteriorly** (anatomical position).
**Circumduction** is a circular movement that is a combination of flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction.
**Dorsiflexion** describes flexion at the ankle joint, lifting the toes toward the shin.
**Plantarflexion** describes bending the foot and toes toward the ground.
**Inversion** moves the sole of the foot toward the median plane.
**Eversion** moves the sole of the foot away from the median plane.
**Opposition** is the movement by which the pad of the **1st digit (thumb)** is brought to another digit pad.
**Protrusion** is a forward movement (e.g., sticking out the mandible), while **Retraction** is a backward movement.
**Elevation** raises a part superiorly (e.g., shrugging shoulders), while **Depression** lowers a part.
**Surface Anatomy** is the study of the external features of the body as they relate to deeper structures.
**Radiological Anatomy** (Imaging Anatomy) involves the study of the structure of the body using X-rays, CT, MRI, and Ultrasound.
**Clinical Anatomy** emphasizes aspects of body structure and function important in the practice of medicine and surgery.
**Systemic Anatomy** is the study of the body's organ systems (e.g., skeletal, muscular, nervous systems).
**Regional Anatomy** (Topographical Anatomy) considers the organization of the body by major parts (e.g., head, neck, thorax).
**Applied Anatomy** is the application of anatomical knowledge to the diagnosis and treatment of disease.
The **Pre-axial** border of the limb corresponds to the **radial** side in the upper limb and the **tibial** side in the lower limb.
The **Post-axial** border of the limb corresponds to the **ulnar** side in the upper limb and the **fibular** side in the lower limb.
**Long Bones** consist of a shaft (**diaphysis**) and two ends (**epiphyses**), exemplified by the **humerus** and **femur**.
**Short Bones** are cuboidal in shape and are found only in the **carpus** (wrist) and **tarsus** (ankle).
**Flat Bones** usually serve protective functions and consist of two plates of compact bone with intervening spongy bone (**diploe**), such as the **cranium**.
**Irregular Bones** have various shapes other than long, short, or flat, such as the **vertebrae** and **hip bones**.
**Sesamoid Bones** develop in certain **tendons** where they cross the ends of long bones to protect the tendon from excessive wear (e.g., **patella**).
**Pneumatic Bones** contain air-filled cavities (sinuses), which make the bone light and aid in resonance (e.g., **maxilla**, **ethmoid**).
**Wormian (Sutural) Bones** are small, irregular bones found within the **sutures** of the cranium.
**Heterotopic Bones** are bones that sometimes form in soft tissues where they are not normally present (e.g., **rider's bone** in the thigh).
The **Diaphysis** is the primary center of ossification and forms the **shaft** of a long bone.
The **Epiphysis** is the secondary center of ossification found at the ends of a long bone.
The **Metaphysis** is the part of the diaphysis nearest to the epiphysis; it is the zone of **active growth**.
The **Epiphyseal Plate** is a layer of **hyaline cartilage** that allows the bone to grow in length; it eventually disappears (synostosis).
**Pressure Epiphyses** are located at the ends of bones and transmit the weight of the body (e.g., head of femur).
**Traction Epiphyses** are associated with the attachment of tendons and are subjected to traction (e.g., **greater trochanter** of femur).
**Atavistic Epiphyses** represent a bone that was phylogenetically independent but is now fused (e.g., **coracoid process** of scapula).
**Aberrant Epiphyses** are not always present, such as the epiphysis at the base of the **second metacarpal**.
**Intramembranous Ossification** occurs when mesenchymal bone models form during the embryonic period and ossify directly (e.g., **flat bones of skull**).
**Endochondral Ossification** involves a cartilage model that is subsequently replaced by bone (e.g., **long bones**).
The **Primary Center of Ossification** usually appears before birth in the center of the diaphysis.
The **Secondary Center of Ossification** usually appears after birth in the epiphyses.
The **Rule of Ossification** states that the center which appears first is usually the **last to fuse** with the shaft (except in the lower end of the fibula).
The **Nutrient Artery** enters the shaft via the **nutrient foramen**, directed away from the **growing end**.
The growing end is the end of the bone where the **epiphysis fuses later**; in the upper limb, these are the **shoulder** and **wrist** ends.
In the lower limb, the growing ends are the **knee** ends (distal femur and proximal tibia).
The **Periosteum** is a fibrous connective tissue membrane covering the bone surface, rich in **sensory nerves** and **osteoblasts**.
The **Periosteal Arteries** supply the compact bone; stripping the periosteum can lead to **bone necrosis**.
**Metaphyseal and Epiphyseal Arteries** arise from the arteries supplying the joint and are crucial for the growth plate.
**Bone Marrow** is found in the **medullary cavity**; red marrow is hematopoietic, while yellow marrow is fatty.
**Compact Bone** (Cortical) provides strength and forms the outer shell, whereas **Spongy Bone** (Cancellous) contains trabeculae.
A **Greenstick Fracture** is an incomplete break where one side of the bone is broken and the other is bent (common in children).
A **Comminuted Fracture** is one where the bone is broken into **several fragments**.
A **Compound (Open) Fracture** involves a break in the bone that also pierces the skin.
**Fracture Healing** involves the formation of a **procallus** (blood clot), then a **fibrocartilaginous callus**, and finally a **bony callus**.
**Osteoblasts** are cells responsible for bone formation, while **Osteoclasts** are responsible for bone resorption.
**Osteocytes** are mature bone cells trapped within the **lacunae** of the bone matrix.
**Osteoporosis** is a clinical condition characterized by a reduction in bone density, increasing fracture risk.
**Rickets** is a disease in children caused by Vitamin D deficiency, leading to **softening of bones**.
**Osteomalacia** is the adult equivalent of rickets, characterized by inadequate mineralization of the bone matrix.
**Avascular Necrosis** is the death of bone tissue due to loss of blood supply (common in the **scaphoid** bone).
**Wolf’s Law** states that bone grows or remodels in response to the **mechanical forces** or stresses placed upon it.
**Cartilage** is a resilient, semi-rigid form of connective tissue that forms parts of the skeleton where flexibility is required.
Cartilage is **avascular**, obtaining nutrients by diffusion from the surrounding **perichondrium** or synovial fluid.
**Hyaline Cartilage** is the most common type, possessing a matrix of **Type II collagen** fibers and a pearly-bluish appearance.
Examples of Hyaline Cartilage include the **articular surfaces** of joints, **costal cartilages**, and the **trachea**.
**Elastic Cartilage** contains numerous **elastic fibers** in its matrix, providing high flexibility.
Examples of Elastic Cartilage include the **auricle of the ear**, **epiglottis**, and **auditory tube**.
**Fibrocartilage** contains thick bundles of **Type I collagen** and is designed to withstand heavy pressure and tension.
Examples of Fibrocartilage include **intervertebral discs**, **pubic symphysis**, and **menisci** of the knee.
**Perichondrium** is a layer of dense irregular connective tissue surrounding most cartilage; it is absent in **fibrocartilage** and **articular hyaline cartilage**.
Cartilage has a **limited capacity for repair**; if damaged, it is often replaced by fibrous tissue or bone.
**Chondrocytes** are the mature cells of cartilage located within spaces called **lacunae**.
**Chondroblasts** are the immature cells that secrete the extracellular matrix of cartilage.
The matrix of cartilage is rich in **glycosaminoglycans (GAGs)** and **proteoglycans**, which bind water and provide resilience.
In old age, **hyaline cartilage** has a tendency to **calcify** or even ossify.
Articular cartilage lacks a **perichondrium**, which allows for a smooth, low-friction surface for joint movement.
The **Costal Cartilages** connect the ribs to the sternum, providing elasticity to the thoracic cage.
**Appositional Growth** of cartilage occurs when new layers are added to the surface by the perichondrium.
**Interstitial Growth** occurs when chondrocytes within the lacunae divide and secrete more matrix, expanding the cartilage from within.
Hyaline cartilage forms the **temporary skeleton** of the fetus which is later replaced by bone.
Elastic cartilage **does not calcify** with age, unlike hyaline cartilage.
Fibrocartilage acts as a **shock absorber** due to its high tensile strength.
The **intervertebral disc** consists of an outer **annulus fibrosus** (fibrocartilage) and an inner **nucleus pulposus**.
A **herniated disc** occurs when the nucleus pulposus protrudes through the annulus fibrosus, often compressing spinal nerves.
**Osteoarthritis** involves the progressive wearing away of **articular hyaline cartilage**.
**Cartilage is aneural**, meaning it lacks a nerve supply; pain from joint disease usually arises from the joint capsule or bone.
The translucent appearance of hyaline cartilage is due to its fibers having a **refractive index** similar to the ground substance.
**White fibrocartilage** is the only type of cartilage that contains **Type I collagen**.
The **fetal skeleton** is initially composed entirely of **hyaline cartilage** models.
Cartilage is capable of rapid growth, which is why it is used for the **embryonic skeleton** and **epiphyseal plates**.
The **epiglottis** must be flexible to close the laryngeal inlet during swallowing, hence it is composed of **elastic cartilage**.
A **Joint (Articulation)** is a site where two or more bones or rigid parts of the skeleton come together.
**Fibrous Joints** are united by fibrous tissue; the degree of movement depends on the length of the fibers.
**Sutures** are fibrous joints found only in the **skull**, where bones are held together by a thin layer of dense fibrous tissue.
**Syndesmosis** is a fibrous joint where bones are joined by a sheet of fibrous tissue (e.g., **interosseous membrane** between radius and ulna).
**Gomphosis** (Dentoalveolar syndesmosis) is a specialized fibrous joint between a tooth and the alveolar process of the jaw.
**Cartilaginous Joints** are united by hyaline cartilage or fibrocartilage.
**Primary Cartilaginous Joints (Synchondroses)** are united by hyaline cartilage and are usually temporary (e.g., **epiphyseal plate**).
**Secondary Cartilaginous Joints (Symphyses)** are strong, slightly mobile joints united by **fibrocartilage** (e.g., **pubic symphysis**, intervertebral discs).
**Synovial Joints** are the most common type and provide free movement; they are characterized by a **joint cavity**.
The **Joint Capsule** consists of an outer **fibrous capsule** and an inner **synovial membrane**.
The **Synovial Membrane** secretes **synovial fluid**, which lubricates the joint and provides nutrients to articular cartilage.
**Articular Cartilage** (usually hyaline) covers the articulating surfaces of bones in a synovial joint.
**Plane Joints** permit gliding or sliding movements (e.g., **acromioclavicular joint**).
**Hinge Joints (Ginglymus)** permit flexion and extension only (e.g., **elbow joint**).
**Saddle Joints** are biaxial and permit movement in two planes (e.g., **carpometacarpal joint of the thumb**).
**Condyloid (Ellipsoid) Joints** permit flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction (e.g., **metacarpophalangeal joints**).
**Ball and Socket Joints** are multiaxial, permitting movement in multiple axes (e.g., **hip joint**, **shoulder joint**).
**Pivot Joints** permit rotation around a central axis (e.g., **atlanto-axial joint**).
**Hilton’s Law** states that the nerves supplying a joint also supply the **muscles moving the joint** and the skin covering their distal attachments.
**Ligaments** are cords or bands of fibrous tissue that connect bone to bone, providing stability.
**Intrinsic Ligaments** are thickenings of the fibrous joint capsule.
**Extrinsic Ligaments** are separate from the capsule, either outside (extracapsular) or inside (intracapsular).
The **Stability of a Synovial Joint** depends on: 1. Shape of bones, 2. Strength of ligaments, and 3. **Muscle tone** (most important).
**Articular Discs (Menisci)** are pads of fibrocartilage found in some synovial joints to improve fit (e.g., **knee joint**).
**Labrum** is a fibrocartilaginous lip that deepens the socket of a ball and socket joint (e.g., **glenoid labrum**).
**Dislocation (Luxation)** is the complete displacement of articulating surfaces of the bones in a joint.
**Subluxation** is a partial or incomplete dislocation of a joint.
**Sprain** is an injury to a **ligament** caused by overstretching or tearing.
**Strain** is an injury to a **muscle or tendon** due to overstretching.
**Arthritis** is inflammation of a joint, which can be inflammatory (Rheumatoid) or degenerative (Osteoarthritis).
**Ankylosis** is the stiffness or fixation of a joint due to disease or surgery.
**Bursae** are small sacs filled with synovial fluid that reduce friction between moving parts.
The **Joint Cavity** is a potential space containing a very small amount of synovial fluid.
**Proprioception** is the sense of the relative position of body parts, provided by sensory nerves in the joint capsule.
**Synovial joints** are highly vascularized by **articular arteries** that form anastomoses around the joint.
**Sympathetic nerves** in joints are primarily vasomotor, controlling blood flow to the joint tissues.
**Symphyses** are always located in the **midline** of the body.
The **manubriosternal joint** is a secondary cartilaginous joint.
The **first sternocostal joint** is a primary cartilaginous joint (synchondrosis).
**Synostosis** is the fusion of two bones (e.g., after the epiphyseal plate disappears).
The **Skin** is the largest organ of the body, consisting of the **Epidermis** (superficial) and **Dermis** (deep).
The **Epidermis** is an avascular layer of **stratified squamous keratinized epithelium**.
The five layers of the epidermis (from deep to superficial) are: Stratum **basale**, **spinosum**, **granulosum**, **lucidum**, and **corneum**.
**Stratum Lucidum** is found only in **thick skin** (palms and soles).
**Melanocytes** in the stratum basale produce **melanin**, which protects against UV radiation.
**Keratinocytes** are the primary cell type in the epidermis, producing the protective protein **keratin**.
The **Dermis** is a vascular layer of dense irregular connective tissue containing collagen and elastic fibers.
The dermis has two layers: the superficial **papillary layer** and the deep **reticular layer**.
**Dermal Papillae** are finger-like projections that interlock with the epidermis, increasing surface area for nutrient exchange.
**Tension Lines (Langer's Lines)** are the direction of collagen fibers in the dermis; incisions parallel to these lines heal with minimal scarring.
**Hair** is an appendage of the skin composed of keratinized cells; the **hair follicle** is an invagination of the epidermis into the dermis.
The **Arrector Pili Muscle** is a bundle of smooth muscle that causes hair to stand up (**goosebumps**) and compresses sebaceous glands.
**Sebaceous Glands** secrete an oily substance called **sebum** into hair follicles to lubricate the skin.
**Sweat (Sudoriferous) Glands** are of two types: **Eccrine** (thermoregulation) and **Apocrine** (scented, found in axilla/groin).
**Nails** are plates of tightly packed, hard, keratinized epidermal cells.
The **Nail Matrix** is the site of nail growth, located proximal to the nail root.
**Superficial Fascia (Hypodermis)** is a layer of loose connective tissue and fat that connects skin to the underlying deep fascia.
Functions of superficial fascia include **insulation**, fat storage, and protection of superficial vessels/nerves.
**Deep Fascia** is a dense, organized connective tissue layer that invests muscles and structures.
**Intermuscular Septa** are extensions of deep fascia that divide muscles into functional groups or compartments.
**Retinacula** are localized thickenings of deep fascia that hold tendons in place at joints (e.g., wrist, ankle).
**Skin Burns** are classified by depth: **1st degree** (epidermis only), **2nd degree** (epidermis + superficial dermis), **3rd degree** (full thickness).
The **Rule of Nines** is used to estimate the surface area of the body affected by burns.
A **Sebaceous Cyst** is caused by the blockage of a sebaceous gland duct, leading to sebum accumulation.
**Skin Grafting** involves moving skin from a donor site to a recipient site to treat wounds or burns.
**Albinism** is a genetic condition characterized by the absence of melanin production.
**Cyanosis** is a bluish discoloration of the skin due to high levels of deoxygenated hemoglobin in the blood.
**Jaundice** is a yellowish discoloration of the skin and sclera caused by excess **bilirubin**.
**Fingerprints** (dermatoglyphs) are formed by the patterns of epidermal ridges on the fingertips.
**Skin Ligaments** (Skin tethers) are fibrous bands that connect the dermis to the deep fascia; they are well-developed in the breast (**Ligaments of Cooper**).
The **Eponychium** (cuticle) is the narrow band of epidermis that extends from the nail wall.
**Keloids** are hypertrophied scars that extend beyond the boundaries of the original wound.
**Decubitus Ulcers** (Pressure sores) occur due to prolonged ischemia in skin overlying bony prominences.
**Hair growth** occurs in cycles: **Anagen** (growth), **Catagen** (involution), and **Telogen** (rest).
The **Hypodermis** contains the **cutaneous nerves** and superficial veins.
**Basal Cell Carcinoma** is the most common form of skin cancer, arising from the stratum basale.
**Malignant Melanoma** is a dangerous form of skin cancer arising from melanocytes.
**Skin is an endocrine organ**, as it synthesizes **Vitamin D** in response to UV light.
**Stretch Marks** (Striae) occur when the dermis is overstretched, damaging collagen fibers.
The **Nail Bed** is the skin beneath the nail plate.
**Skeletal Muscle** is striated, voluntary, and makes up the bulk of the body's muscle mass.
**Smooth Muscle** is non-striated, involuntary, and found in the walls of hollow organs and blood vessels.
**Cardiac Muscle** is striated, involuntary, and found exclusively in the **myocardium** (heart wall).
**Somatic Muscles** are those that move the body wall (skeletal), while **Visceral Muscles** are found in organs (smooth/cardiac).
**Red Muscle Fibers** (Type I) are slow-twitch, rich in myoglobin/mitochondria, and resistant to fatigue.
**White Muscle Fibers** (Type II) are fast-twitch, have less myoglobin, and are adapted for rapid, powerful contractions.
A **Motor Unit** consists of a single motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates.
The **Origin** of a muscle is usually the proximal, fixed attachment, while the **Insertion** is the distal, mobile attachment.
The **Fleshy Belly** of a muscle is the contractile part, while the **Tendon** is the non-contractile fibrous part.
**Aponeurosis** is a broad, flat sheet of fibrous tissue that serves as a muscle attachment.
**Parallel Muscles** have fibers running parallel to the long axis (e.g., **sartorius**).
**Pennate Muscles** have fibers that run obliquely to the tendon, like a feather; they are stronger but have less range of motion.
**Unipennate** fibers attach to one side of the tendon (e.g., **extensor digitorum longus**).
**Bipennate** fibers attach to both sides of a central tendon (e.g., **rectus femoris**).
**Multipennate** fibers converge from several directions toward a tendon (e.g., **deltoid**).
**Fusiform Muscles** are spindle-shaped with a thick belly and tapered ends (e.g., **biceps brachii**).
**Convergent Muscles** have a broad origin and converge to a single tendon (e.g., **pectoralis major**).
A **Prime Mover (Agonist)** is the main muscle responsible for a specific movement.
An **Antagonist** is a muscle that opposes the action of the prime mover.
A **Synergist** complements the action of the prime mover by assisting or eliminating unwanted movements.
A **Fixator** stabilizes the origin of the prime mover so it can act more efficiently.
**Muscle Tone** is a state of partial contraction even when the muscle is at rest.
**Hypertrophy** is an increase in the size of muscle fibers (not number) in response to exercise.
**Atrophy** is a decrease in muscle size due to disuse or loss of nerve supply.
**Hemiplegia** is paralysis of one side of the body (e.g., right arm and right leg).
**Paraplegia** is paralysis of both lower limbs, usually due to spinal cord injury.
**Quadriplegia** (Tetraplegia) is paralysis of all four limbs.
**Hemiparesis** is weakness (rather than total paralysis) on one side of the body.
**Synovial Bursae** are closed sacs of serous membrane that minimize friction where tendons rub against bone.
**Tendon Sheaths** are tubular bursae that surround tendons as they pass under retinacula.
**Cardiac Muscle** contains **intercalated discs**, which allow for rapid spread of electrical impulses.
**Smooth Muscle** is regulated by the **Autonomic Nervous System** and hormones.
**Skeletal Muscle** is innervated by **somatic motor nerves**.
The **Neuromuscular Junction** is the site of communication between a motor neuron and a muscle fiber.
**Myasthenia Gravis** is an autoimmune disease where antibodies block acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction.
**Rigidity** is an increase in muscle tone regardless of the speed of movement (common in Parkinson’s).
**Spasticity** is a velocity-dependent increase in muscle tone (common in upper motor neuron lesions).
**Circular Muscles** (Sphincters) surround body openings and close them upon contraction (e.g., **orbicularis oculi**).
**Reflex Contraction** occurs automatically (e.g., the knee-jerk reflex).
**Shunt Muscles** act parallel to the bone and help prevent dislocation at a joint (e.g., **brachioradialis**).
The **Vascular System** consists of the heart and blood vessels (cardiovascular) and the lymphatic system.
**Systemic Circulation** carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle to the body and returns deoxygenated blood to the right atrium.
**Pulmonary Circulation** carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and returns oxygenated blood to the left atrium.
**Arteries** carry blood away from the heart and generally have thicker walls than veins.
**Arterioles** are the smallest arteries and are the primary site of **peripheral resistance**.
**Capillaries** are microscopic vessels where the exchange of gases, nutrients, and wastes occurs between blood and tissues.
**Veins** carry blood toward the heart; they have thinner walls, larger lumens, and often contain **valves** to prevent backflow.
**Venules** are small veins that drain blood from capillary beds.
**Anastomosis** is a communication between two vessels, providing **collateral circulation** if one vessel is blocked.
**Arteriovenous Anastomosis (AVA)** allows blood to bypass capillary beds, directly connecting arterioles to venules (important in thermoregulation).
**End Arteries** are those that do not anastomose with neighboring arteries; their occlusion leads to tissue death (**infarction**).
Examples of **Anatomical End Arteries** include the **central artery of the retina**.
**Functional End Arteries** have small anastomoses that are insufficient to provide collateral flow (e.g., **coronary arteries**).
**Sinusoids** are wide, leaky capillaries found in the **liver**, spleen, and bone marrow.
**Portal System** is a sequence where blood passes through two capillary beds before returning to the heart (e.g., **Hepatic Portal System**).
The **Lymphatic System** drains excess **tissue fluid (lymph)** back into the venous system.
**Lymph** is the clear, colorless fluid collected from interstitial spaces.
**Lymphatic Capillaries** are blind-ended tubes that are more permeable than blood capillaries.
**Lymph Nodes** are small, bean-shaped organs that filter lymph and house lymphocytes.
**Lymphatics** (Lymphatic vessels) carry lymph toward the heart, eventually draining into the **thoracic duct** or right lymphatic duct.
The **Thoracic Duct** drains lymph from most of the body (except the upper right quadrant) into the left venous angle.
**Lymphoid Tissues** include the spleen, thymus, tonsils, and Peyer's patches.
**Lymphangitis** is the inflammation of lymphatic vessels, often seen as red streaks on the skin.
**Lymphadenitis** is the inflammation and enlargement of lymph nodes due to infection.
**Lymphedema** is swelling caused by the accumulation of lymph in tissues due to lymphatic obstruction.
**Vasa Vasorum** are small blood vessels that supply the walls of large arteries and veins.
**Venous Valves** are particularly numerous in the veins of the **lower limbs** to aid in venous return against gravity.
**Musculovenous Pump** describes the mechanism where muscle contraction compresses veins, aiding blood flow toward the heart.
**Arteriosclerosis** is the hardening and loss of elasticity of the arterial walls.
**Atherosclerosis** is a form of arteriosclerosis characterized by the buildup of fatty plaques (atheromas).
**Varicose Veins** are dilated, tortuous veins caused by incompetent valves.
**Thrombosis** is the formation of a blood clot within a blood vessel.
**Embolism** is the blockage of a vessel by a detached clot or foreign material (e.g., air, fat).
**Aneurysm** is a localized, abnormal dilation of a blood vessel, usually an artery.
**Capillary Beds** are regulated by **precapillary sphincters**.
**Large Arteries** (Elastic) include the aorta; they serve as pressure reservoirs.
**Medium Arteries** (Muscular) distribute blood to organs and can regulate flow via vasoconstriction.
**Deep Veins** usually travel in pairs alongside arteries, a phenomenon known as **Venae Comitantes**.
The **Right Lymphatic Duct** drains lymph from the right side of the head, neck, thorax, and the right upper limb.
**Lacteals** are specialized lymphatic capillaries in the small intestine that absorb dietary fats (chyle).
A **Neuron** is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system, consisting of a **cell body (soma)**, **dendrites**, and an **axon**.
**Dendrites** carry impulses toward the cell body, while the **Axon** carries impulses away from the cell body.
**Multipolar Neurons** have one axon and multiple dendrites; they are the most common type (e.g., motor neurons).
**Bipolar Neurons** have one axon and one dendrite (e.g., in the **retina** and olfactory epithelium).
**Pseudounipolar Neurons** have a single short process that divides into two branches; found in the **dorsal root ganglia**.
**Sensory (Afferent) Neurons** carry information from receptors to the CNS.
**Motor (Efferent) Neurons** carry instructions from the CNS to effectors (muscles/glands).
**Interneurons** (Association neurons) are located entirely within the CNS and connect sensory and motor neurons.
The **Central Nervous System (CNS)** consists of the **Brain** and **Spinal Cord**.
The **Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)** consists of **Cranial Nerves**, **Spinal Nerves**, and **Ganglia**.
**Neuroglia** (Glial cells) are non-neuronal supporting cells that are 5-10 times more numerous than neurons.
**Astrocytes** (CNS) provide structural support, form the **blood-brain barrier**, and regulate the chemical environment.
**Oligodendrocytes** (CNS) form the **myelin sheath** around multiple axons in the CNS.
**Microglia** (CNS) are the resident macrophages (phagocytic cells) of the nervous system.
**Ependymal Cells** (CNS) line the ventricles of the brain and produce **Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF)**.
**Schwann Cells** (PNS) form the **myelin sheath** around a single axon in the PNS.
**Satellite Cells** (PNS) surround neuron cell bodies in ganglia, providing physical and metabolic support.
There are **12 pairs of Cranial Nerves** (CN I-XII) originating from the brain.
**CN I (Olfactory)** and **CN II (Optic)** are purely sensory; **CN X (Vagus)** has the most extensive distribution.
There are **31 pairs of Spinal Nerves**: 8 Cervical, 12 Thoracic, 5 Lumbar, 5 Sacral, and 1 Coccygeal.
Spinal nerves are formed by the union of a **dorsal (sensory) root** and a **ventral (motor) root**.
A **Dermatome** is the area of skin supplied by a single spinal nerve.
A **Myotome** is the group of muscles supplied by a single spinal nerve.
**Nerve Plexuses** are networks formed by the mixing of **anterior rami** of spinal nerves (e.g., Brachial, Lumbar).
The **Somatic Nervous System** provides voluntary control of skeletal muscles and carries sensory info from skin/joints.
The **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)** is involuntary and regulates smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.
The **Sympathetic Division** (Thoracolumbar) is responsible for the **'Fight or Flight'** response.
The **Parasympathetic Division** (Craniosacral) is responsible for the **'Rest and Digest'** response.
**Receptors** are structures that detect changes in the environment (stimuli).
**Mechanoreceptors** respond to physical deformation (touch, pressure, vibration).
**Nociceptors** are receptors for **pain**, responding to tissue damage.
**Proprioceptors** provide information about body position and movement.
**Effectors** are muscles or glands that respond to motor impulses.
**Gray Matter** in the CNS contains neuron cell bodies and dendrites.
**White Matter** in the CNS contains primarily myelinated axons.
**Ganglion** is a collection of neuron cell bodies located outside the CNS.
**Nucleus** is a collection of neuron cell bodies located inside the CNS.
**Synapse** is the site of communication between two neurons or between a neuron and an effector cell.
**Neurotransmitters** are chemical messengers (e.g., Acetylcholine, Norepinephrine) released at synapses.
The **Reflex Arc** consists of a receptor, sensory neuron, integration center, motor neuron, and effector.
**Homeostasis** is defined as the maintenance of nearly constant conditions in the **Internal Environment**.
The **Internal Environment** of the body refers specifically to the **Extracellular Fluid (ECF)**.
**Negative Feedback** mechanisms act to return a variable toward a mean value, effectively **reversing** the initial stimulus.
The **Baroreceptor Reflex** is a classic example of negative feedback regulating **Arterial Blood Pressure**.
**Positive Feedback** mechanisms lead to instability and often a 'vicious cycle,' but are useful in specific physiological events like **Childbirth (Oxytocin)** and **Blood Clotting**.
The generation of an **Action Potential** in a nerve fiber is a physiological example of **Positive Feedback** via sodium channel opening.
**Feed-forward control** (Adaptive control) occurs in the **Cerebellum** to allow for smooth, rapid muscle movements without delayed feedback.
**Extracellular Fluid (ECF)** contains high concentrations of **Sodium (Na+)**, **Chloride (Cl-)**, and **Bicarbonate**.
**Intracellular Fluid (ICF)** contains high concentrations of **Potassium (K+)**, **Magnesium**, and **Phosphate**.
Normal body temperature is typically cited as **98.6°F (37°C)**, regulated by the **Hypothalamus**.
The **Anterior Hypothalamic-Preoptic Area** serves as the primary **Thermostat** or heat-control center.
**Heat production** is primarily a byproduct of **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR)** and muscle activity (shivering).
**Heat loss** occurs via **Radiation (60%)**, **Evaporation (22%)**, **Conduction**, and **Convection**.
**Pyrogens** cause the hypothalamic set-point to rise, leading to **Fever**.
The **Cell Membrane** is a **Lipid Bilayer** composed primarily of **Phospholipids**, **Sphingolipids**, and **Cholesterol**.
**Cholesterol** in the membrane determines the degree of **Permeability** to water-soluble constituents and controls **Fluidity**.
**Integral Proteins** span the entire membrane and act as **Channels**, **Transporters**, or **Receptors**.
**Peripheral Proteins** are attached to only one surface and often function as **Enzymes** or controllers of channel transport.
The **Glycocalyx** is a carbohydrate coat on the outside of the cell that provides a **Negative Charge**, aids in **Cell-Cell Attachment**, and acts as **Receptors**.
**Membranous Organelles** include the **Mitochondria**, **Endoplasmic Reticulum**, **Golgi Apparatus**, **Lysosomes**, and **Peroxisomes**.
**Non-membranous Organelles** include **Ribosomes**, **Centrioles**, and **Cytoskeleton** components.
**Self-replicative Organelles** include **Mitochondria** and **Peroxisomes**, as they contain their own DNA or replicate by fission.
**Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER)** is studded with **Ribosomes** and is the primary site of **Protein Synthesis**.
**Smooth Endoplasmic Reticulum (SER)** is the site of **Lipid Synthesis** and **Calcium Storage** (especially in muscle as sarcoplasmic reticulum).
The **Golgi Apparatus** functions as the 'packaging plant,' processing substances from the ER into **Secretory Vesicles**.
**Lysosomes** contain **Acid Hydrolases** used for intracellular digestion of damaged structures and bacteria.
**Peroxisomes** contain **Oxidases** and **Catalase**; they are vital for the **Beta-oxidation of long-chain fatty acids** and detoxification of alcohol.
The **Cytoskeleton** consists of **Microtubules** (Tubulin), **Microfilaments** (Actin), and **Intermediate Filaments**.
**Endocytosis** includes **Pinocytosis** (cell drinking) and **Phagocytosis** (cell eating).
**Pinocytosis** is the only way most large macromolecules, such as **Proteins**, can enter the cell, requiring **ATP** and **Calcium**.
**Simple Diffusion** occurs through the membrane or through channels without a carrier and is NOT **Saturable**.
**Facilitated Diffusion** requires a **Carrier Protein** and exhibits **Vmax (Saturation)** kinetics.
**Primary Active Transport** uses energy directly from **ATP hydrolysis** (e.g., **Na+/K+ ATPase**).
**Secondary Active Transport** uses the energy stored in a **Concentration Gradient** (usually Sodium) to move another substance.
**Co-transport (Symport)** moves two substances in the same direction (e.g., **Sodium-Glucose** in renal tubules).
**Counter-transport (Antiport)** moves substances in opposite directions (e.g., **Sodium-Calcium exchanger**).
**Voltage-gated Channels** open in response to changes in **Electrical Potential** (e.g., Voltage-gated Sodium channels).
**Ligand-gated Channels** open when a chemical binder attaches (e.g., **Acetylcholine receptor**).
**Tetrodotoxin (TTX)** is a potent blocker of **Voltage-gated Sodium channels**.
**Tetraethylammonium (TEA)** acts as a blocker of **Potassium channels**.
The **Na+/K+ ATPase Pump** pumps **3 Na+ ions out** and **2 K+ ions in**, maintaining a **Negative Resting Membrane Potential** (Electrogenic).
**Blood Functions** include **Transport** of oxygen/nutrients, **Regulation** of pH/temperature, and **Protection** via clotting/immunity.
**Composition of Blood** consists of approximately **55% Plasma** and **45% Formed Elements** (hematocrit).
**Plasma Proteins** are classified into **Albumin**, **Globulins**, and **Fibrinogen**.
**Albumin** (60% of total) is the smallest and most abundant plasma protein, synthesized in the **Liver**.
The primary role of **Albumin** is to maintain **Colloid Osmotic Pressure (Oncotic Pressure)**, preventing fluid leak from capillaries.
**Globulins** are divided into alpha, beta, and gamma; **Gamma Globulins** are **Antibodies** produced by plasma cells.
**Fibrinogen** is essential for **Blood Coagulation**, converting to **Fibrin** during the clotting process.
**Edema** is the accumulation of excess fluid in the **Interstitial Spaces**.
Pathophysiology of edema includes **Decreased Plasma Oncotic Pressure** (e.g., Hypoalbuminemia in liver disease or Nephrotic Syndrome).
**Increased Capillary Hydrostatic Pressure** (e.g., Heart Failure) is a major cause of generalized edema.
**Increased Capillary Permeability** due to toxins or histamine leads to localized edema.
**Lymphatic Obstruction** (Lymphedema) prevents the return of fluid and proteins to the circulation.
**Serum** is defined as plasma minus **Clotting Factors (Fibrinogen)**.
Normal **Plasma Osmolarity** is approximately **280-300 mOsm/L**.
The **Donnan Effect** describes the slight extra osmotic pressure in plasma caused by proteins attracting cations like **Sodium**.
Total blood volume in a healthy adult is roughly **8% of body weight** (approx 5 liters).
**Hypoproteinemia** leads to a drop in oncotic pressure, causing a shift of fluid from the intravascular to the **Interstitial Compartment**.
**Kwashiorkor** is a clinical condition of protein deficiency where severe **Ascites** and edema occur due to low albumin.
**Starling's Forces** govern the movement of fluid across the capillary membrane.
**Anasarca** is the medical term for severe, generalized edema.
**Red Blood Cells (RBCs)** are **Biconcave Disks** with a diameter of **7.8 micrometers**, allowing for high surface-area-to-volume ratio.
RBCs lack a **Nucleus**, **Mitochondria**, and **Ribosomes**, making them unable to synthesize proteins or perform oxidative phosphorylation.
In the first few weeks of gestation, RBCs are produced in the **Yolk Sac**.
During the second trimester, the **Liver** is the primary site of erythropoiesis, with the **Spleen** and **Lymph nodes** contributing.
From the third trimester onwards and through adulthood, the **Red Bone Marrow** is the sole site of RBC production.
By age 20, the marrow of **Long Bones** (except proximal humerus/tibia) becomes fatty (**Yellow Marrow**) and ceases production.
In adults, RBCs are produced mainly in the **Membranous Bones** (Vertebrae, Sternum, Ribs, Ilium).
The **Pluripotential Hematopoietic Stem Cell (PHSC)** is the precursor to all blood cell lines.

The stages of erythropoiesis: **Proerythroblast** -> **Basophil Erythroblast** -> **Polychromatophil Erythroblast** -> **Orthochromatic Erythroblast (Normoblast)** -> **Reticulocyte** -> **Erythrocyte**.
The **Nucleus is extruded** at the **Orthochromatic Erythroblast** stage to form a **Reticulocyte**.
**Reticulocytes** contain remnants of **Golgi Apparatus** and **RNA**, and usually enter the blood where they mature in 1-2 days.
The **Reticulocyte Count** is a clinical marker for **Bone Marrow Activity**.
**Hypoxia** (low oxygen) is the most potent stimulus for RBC production.
**Erythropoietin (EPO)** is a hormone that stimulates the production of **Proerythroblasts** from stem cells.
**90% of Erythropoietin** is produced in the **Kidneys** (peritubular interstitial cells); the remaining 10% is produced in the **Liver**.
Patients with **Chronic Renal Failure** often suffer from severe anemia due to **EPO deficiency**.
**Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)** and **Folic Acid** are essential for **DNA Synthesis** (specifically Thymidine Triphosphate).
Deficiency in B12 or Folic Acid leads to **Maturation Failure**, resulting in **Megaloblastic Anemia** with large, fragile **Macrocytes**.
**Intrinsic Factor**, secreted by **Parietal Cells** of the stomach, is required for the absorption of **Vitamin B12** in the **Ileum**.
**Pernicious Anemia** is caused by the lack of **Intrinsic Factor**, leading to B12 malabsorption.
Hemoglobin synthesis begins in the **Proerythroblast** and continues into the **Reticulocyte** stage.
**Hemoglobin A (HbA)** is the adult form, consisting of **2 Alpha and 2 Beta chains**.
**Hemoglobin F (HbF)** is fetal hemoglobin, consisting of **2 Alpha and 2 Gamma chains**, having a higher affinity for oxygen.
**Hemoglobin A2 (HbA2)** is a minor adult form (2-3%) consisting of **2 Alpha and 2 Delta chains**.
Each molecule of Hemoglobin can carry up to **4 molecules of Oxygen** (8 atoms).
**Iron** is essential for Hb formation; it is absorbed in the **Duodenum** and transported in the blood by **Transferrin**.
Iron is stored in cells (liver/marrow) as **Ferritin**; if storage capacity is exceeded, it forms **Hemosiderin**.
**Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV)** measures the average size of RBCs; normal range is **80-100 fL**.
**Microcytic Anemia** (MCV < 80) is most commonly caused by **Iron Deficiency Anemia**.
**Macrocytic Anemia** (MCV > 100) is caused by **Vitamin B12 or Folate deficiency**.
**Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC)** measures the concentration of Hb in a given volume of packed RBCs (Normal: **32-36%**).
**Hypochromic Anemia** is characterized by a low **MCHC**, often seen in Iron Deficiency.
**Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH)** is the average amount of Hb per RBC (Normal: **27-32 pg**).
**Sickle Cell Anemia** is caused by a point mutation (Valine for Glutamic Acid) in the **Beta chain** of Hemoglobin (**HbS**).
**Thalassemia** is a genetic defect involving the reduced synthesis rate of either alpha or beta globin chains.
In **Iron Deficiency Anemia**, labs show **Low Ferritin**, **High TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)**, and **Low Serum Iron**.
**Methemoglobin** is a form where iron is in the **Ferric (Fe3+)** state rather than Ferrous (Fe2+), making it unable to bind oxygen.
**Polycythemia Vera** is a pathological condition characterized by an excess of RBCs due to a genetic mutation (JAK2).
**Secondary Polycythemia** occurs as an adaptive response to **High Altitude** or chronic lung disease.
**Aplastic Anemia** involves bone marrow failure, resulting in **Pancytopenia** (low RBCs, WBCs, and platelets).
White Blood Cells (Leukocytes) are divided into **Granulocytes** and **Agranulocytes**.
**Granulocytes** include **Neutrophils**, **Eosinophils**, and **Basophils**.
**Agranulocytes** include **Lymphocytes** and **Monocytes**.
**Neutrophils** (60-70% of WBCs) are the 'first responders' to bacterial infection.
**Diapedesis** is the process by which WBCs squeeze through capillary pores to enter tissues.
**Chemotaxis** is the movement of WBCs toward a chemical source (e.g., bacterial toxins, C5a, LTB4).
**Opsonization** is the coating of a pathogen with antibodies or **C3b** to enhance phagocytosis.
**Leukocytosis** is an increase in total WBC count, often seen in acute infections.
**Leukopenia** is a decrease in WBC count, making the body highly susceptible to infections.
**Leukemia** is the uncontrolled production of abnormal WBCs, leading to bone marrow crowding and **Anemia/Thrombocytopenia**.
**Macrophages** are mature **Monocytes** that have entered the tissues; they are larger and more powerful phagocytes than neutrophils.
The **Reticuloendothelial System (RES)** (or Mononuclear Phagocyte System) includes monocytes, mobile macrophages, and fixed macrophages in tissues.
**Kupffer Cells** are specialized macrophages located in the **Liver Sinusoids**.
**Alveolar Macrophages** are the resident phagocytes in the **Lungs**.
**Microglia** are the resident macrophages of the **Central Nervous System**.
**First Line of Defense** in inflammation is the **Tissue Macrophage**.
**Second Line of Defense** is the **Neutrophil Invasion** into the inflamed area.
**Third Line of Defense** is the **Second Macrophage Invasion** (Monocyte migration).
**Fourth Line of Defense** is the increased production of granulocytes and monocytes by the **Bone Marrow**.
The **Walling-off effect** is the delay of bacterial spread via the formation of **Fibrinogen clots** in the inflammation area.
**Basophils** and **Mast Cells** release **Histamine**, **Heparin**, and **Bradykinin**, playing a key role in Type I hypersensitivity.
**Eosinophils** are specialized for defense against **Parasitic Infections** and are elevated in **Allergic Reactions** (Asthma).
Eosinophils release **Major Basic Protein (MBP)** to kill parasites (larvae).
**Pus** is a collection of necrotic tissue, dead neutrophils, and dead macrophages.
**Natural Killer (NK) Cells** are a type of lymphocyte that kills virally infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization.
The **ABO Blood System** is based on the presence of **Antigens (Agglutinogens)** on the RBC surface: A and B.
**Type A** blood has A-antigens and **Anti-B antibodies**.
**Type B** blood has B-antigens and **Anti-A antibodies**.
**Type AB** blood has both A and B antigens and **No antibodies** (Universal Recipient).
**Type O** blood has **No antigens** and both Anti-A and Anti-B antibodies (Universal Donor).
**Landsteiner’s Law** states that if an agglutinogen is present on an RBC, the corresponding agglutinin (antibody) must be absent from the plasma.
**Agglutinins** (Antibodies) of the ABO system are mostly **IgM** and do not cross the placenta.
The **Rh System** is based on the **D-antigen**; people are classified as **Rh-positive** if they have the D-antigen.
Unlike ABO, **Anti-Rh antibodies** are NOT naturally occurring; they only develop after exposure to Rh-positive blood.
**Erythroblastosis Fetalis** (Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn) occurs when an **Rh-negative mother** carries an **Rh-positive fetus**.
In Erythroblastosis Fetalis, the mother's **Anti-D IgG** antibodies cross the placenta and attack fetal RBCs.
**RhoGAM** (anti-D immunoglobulin) is given to Rh-negative mothers to prevent sensitization to the D-antigen.
An **Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction** involves immediate agglutination and hemolysis of donor RBCs, leading to **Jaundice** and **Renal Failure**.
**Agglutination** is the clumping of RBCs, while **Hemolysis** is the actual rupture of the RBC membrane.
The **D-antigen** is the most immunogenic of the Rh group.
Blood typing involves mixing RBCs with known **Antisera** (Anti-A, Anti-B, Anti-D) to observe clumping.
**Cross-matching** is the final check before transfusion where donor RBCs are mixed with recipient serum.
**Hydrops Fetalis** is the most severe form of Erythroblastosis Fetalis, characterized by extreme fetal edema.
**Kernicterus** is brain damage in neonates caused by high levels of **Bilirubin** crossing the blood-brain barrier.
**Type O Negative** is the true universal donor blood because it lacks A, B, and D antigens.
